schools.summary: Gill: the faith here spoken of is not a mere moral virtue (a branch of the law), nor a bare assent to anything revealed in the gospel, nor a faith of doing miracles, nor an implicit one, nor a mere profession, but the true grace of faith; Paul describing faith as the substance of things hoped for and the evidence of things not seen — the twofold nature of faith, giving reality to hope and proof to the unseen (Paul, opening his great chapter on faith, giving its working description: faith 'is the substance of things hoped for,' it gives a present subsistence and reality to the future things we hope for, making them solid and present to the soul as if possessed, and 'the evidence of things not seen,' it is the proof, the convincing demonstration, of unseen realities, so the believer is as sure of them as if he saw them with his eyes, so faith makes the future present and the invisible certain, laying hold of God's promises and the unseen world).

themes: now faith is the substance of things hoped for
the evidence of things not seen
faith giving reality to hope and proof to the unseen
making the future present and the invisible certain

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the elders' are meant not merely old men or elders in age but such as lived in ancient times, some before the flood and to a great age, others in office (civil or ecclesiastical), the ancestors and predecessors of the Hebrews; Paul declaring that by faith the elders obtained a good report — the ancients commended by God through their faith (Paul, before naming his examples, stating the great point they will prove: 'by it,' by this very faith just described, 'the elders obtained a good report,' the men of old, the patriarchs and saints of former ages, were testified of and well reported, approved and commended by God himself on account of their faith, so it was their faith, and what it wrought, that won them God's testimony and an honoured name in the sacred record, the whole roll of witnesses that follows being proof that faith is the thing God commends).

themes: for by it
the elders obtained a good report
the ancients commended by God through faith
faith winning God's testimony and an honoured name

schools.summary: Chrysostom: faith needs a generous and vigorous soul, one rising above all things of sense and passing beyond the weakness of human reasonings, for it is not possible to become a believer otherwise than by raising one's self above the common customs of the world; Paul declaring that through faith we understand the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that the visible was not made of things that appear — the creation by God's word grasped only by faith (Paul giving his first instance of faith's power, the apprehension of creation itself: 'through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God,' it being by faith not by sight or human reasoning that we know the universe was made and ordered by God's bare word of command, 'so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear'; this a witness to creation out of nothing, the visible world not formed out of any pre-existing visible matter but called into being by God's word, so the very origin of all things is held by faith).

debates.question: Out of what were the visible worlds made?

sides.position: Not out of any pre-existing visible matter, but called into being by God's bare word of command — a witness to creation out of nothing, grasped by faith

themes: through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God
things which are seen were not made of things which do appear
creation by God's word grasped by faith
a witness to creation out of nothing

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle proceeds to examples of faith and begins with Abel; that Adam and Eve are not mentioned is not because they were not believers but for other reasons (the fall and ruin, etc.); Paul declaring that by faith Abel offered a more excellent sacrifice than Cain, obtained witness of his righteousness, and being dead yet speaketh — the faith that made Abel's offering accepted and his testimony enduring (Paul beginning the roll with Abel: 'by faith' he 'offered unto God a more excellent sacrifice than Cain,' his offering excelling Cain's not in itself only but because offered in faith, looking to the promised seed and sacrifice, and the fruit twofold: 'by which he obtained witness that he was righteous, God testifying of his gifts,' God himself bearing witness to Abel's acceptance and accounting him righteous through faith, and 'by it he being dead yet speaketh,' for though long dead, Abel's faith still speaks to us, preaching across the ages).

themes: by faith Abel offered unto God a more excellent sacrifice than Cain
by which he obtained witness that he was righteous
by it he being dead yet speaketh
faith winning God's witness, and speaking still

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of a spiritual translation from the power of darkness into the kingdom of Christ (as all converted persons are translated, and doubtless Enoch was), nor of a rapture or removal from one part of the earth to another, but of a bodily translation to heaven without dying; Paul declaring that by faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death, having pleased God before his translation — the faith of Enoch rewarded by escaping death and the testimony that he pleased God (Paul naming Enoch as the second witness: 'by faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death,' taken up bodily to heaven without passing through death, 'and was not found, because God had translated him,' he vanishing from earth, God having taken him, and Paul marking the ground of this honour: 'before his translation he had this testimony, that he pleased God,' so Enoch's walk with God, which was a walk of faith, won him both the testimony of pleasing God and the singular reward of being taken to heaven without death).

themes: by faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death
because God had translated him
he had this testimony, that he pleased God
faith rewarded by translation without death

schools.summary: Gill: without faith it is impossible to please God, or do things well-pleasing in his sight, or any of the duties of religion in an acceptable way (as prayer, praise, attendance on the word and ordinances, or any good works), because such are without Christ and without his Spirit; Paul declaring that without faith it is impossible to please God, for he that comes to God must believe that he is, and that he rewards them that diligently seek him — the indispensable necessity of faith and its two essential objects (Paul drawing from Enoch's case the universal principle: 'without faith it is impossible to please him,' no act of worship or obedience can be acceptable to God apart from faith, and naming the two things the seeker must believe: 'that he is,' that God truly exists, the first article, 'and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him,' that he is gracious to recompense those who earnestly seek him, so faith is the indispensable ground of all true coming to God, resting on his being and his goodness).

themes: but without faith it is impossible to please him
he that cometh to God must believe that he is
that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
faith the ground of all true coming to God

schools.summary: Chrysostom: as the Son of God, speaking of his own coming, said 'in the days of Noah they married and were given in marriage' (Luke 17:26-27), the apostle recalls to their mind an appropriate image, fitting his theme of things not yet seen; Paul declaring that by faith Noah, warned of things not seen, moved with fear, prepared an ark, condemned the world, and became heir of the righteousness by faith — the faith of Noah that built the ark and inherited righteousness (Paul naming Noah: 'being warned of God of things not seen as yet,' the coming flood of which there was no visible sign, Noah, 'moved with fear,' a reverent godly fear taking God's warning to heart, 'prepared an ark to the saving of his house,' by faith building on God's bare word against all appearance, and the results threefold: 'by the which he condemned the world,' his faith and obedience condemning the unbelief of the perishing world, and he 'became heir of the righteousness which is by faith,' an heir of that justifying righteousness which faith receives).

themes: by faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark
by the which he condemned the world
became heir of the righteousness which is by faith
building on God's word against all appearance

schools.summary: Gill: this call is not to be understood of his name (though his first name Abram might be given him in the faith of his being a great man) but of his person, referring to his being called out of Ur of the Chaldees and from his kindred to go to a land God would show him; Paul declaring that by faith Abraham, when called, obeyed and went out, not knowing where he went — the obedience of faith that follows God into the unknown (Paul turning to the great patriarch: 'by faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, obeyed,' at God's bare call leaving his country, kindred, and home for a land not yet shown, 'and he went out, not knowing whither he went'; the greatness of such faith, that Abraham obeyed without seeing the destination, trusting God's word alone, going forth into utter uncertainty because he leaned on the One who called him, so the obedience of faith is to follow God even when the way is unknown, resting on his promise).

themes: by faith Abraham, when he was called to go out... obeyed
a place which he should after receive for an inheritance
he went out, not knowing whither he went
the obedience of faith following God into the unknown

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan is called the land of promise because it was promised to Abraham and his seed, and is typical of heaven, which is not by the works of the law but by the free promise and grace of God; here Abraham sojourned for a while; Paul declaring that by faith Abraham sojourned in the land of promise as in a strange country, dwelling in tents with Isaac and Jacob — the patient faith of a sojourner in the very land that was promised (Paul noting a further trial of faith: even in 'the land of promise,' the land God had given him for an inheritance, Abraham lived 'as in a strange country,' a stranger and foreigner never possessing it, 'dwelling in tabernacles,' tents, the movable homes of a pilgrim, 'with Isaac and Jacob, the heirs with him of the same promise'; the patience of such faith, that Abraham held God's promise firm though he and his son and grandson all lived as homeless wanderers in the land, owning not a foot of it, waiting for what God had pledged).

themes: by faith he sojourned in the land of promise, as in a strange country
dwelling in tabernacles with Isaac and Jacob
the heirs with him of the same promise
patient faith holding the promise though homeless

schools.summary: Gill: this is not the city of Jerusalem (earthly), nor the gospel church state, but the new Jerusalem above said to have twelve foundations, the heavenly city, in which glorious state Abraham, with the rest of the saints being raised from the dead, will dwell in perfect bliss; Paul explaining that Abraham looked for a city which has foundations, whose builder and maker is God — the heavenly hope that made Abraham content to be a pilgrim below (Paul giving the secret of Abraham's patient pilgrim-life: it was because 'he looked for a city which hath foundations,' his faith and hope reaching past the tents and the earthly Canaan to a lasting, permanent city, in contrast to his own moving tents which had no foundations, 'whose builder and maker is God'; Abraham seeking no earthly settlement but the heavenly, abiding city that God himself designs and builds, so the believer's true home is that everlasting city of God, and the hope of it reconciles him to being a stranger here below).

themes: for he looked for a city which hath foundations
whose builder and maker is God
the heavenly city Abraham sought
the hope reconciling him to being a stranger below

schools.summary: Gill: some copies add 'being barren' (so the Vulgate and Oriental versions), a circumstance which makes her faith appear the greater; it is a question whether the apostle speaks of the faith of Sarah or of Abraham, some referring it to him; Paul declaring that through faith Sarah received strength to conceive when past age, because she judged him faithful who had promised — the faith that laid hold of God's faithfulness against natural impossibility (Paul naming Sarah, or as some take it Abraham's faith regarding Sarah: 'through faith also Sara herself received strength to conceive seed, and was delivered of a child when she was past age,' a thing naturally impossible, she being old and barren, and giving the ground of the faith that obtained it: 'because she judged him faithful who had promised'; this the very heart of faith, to rest the impossible promise on the faithfulness of the God who made it, counting that he who promised both can and will perform, however nature contradicts).

debates.question: Is the faith here Sarah's own, or Abraham's?

sides.position: Sarah's own faith — she herself received strength to conceive, judging him faithful who had promised
Abraham's faith regarding Sarah — some refer the believing to him

themes: through faith also Sara herself received strength to conceive seed
was delivered of a child when she was past age
because she judged him faithful who had promised
faith resting the impossible on God's faithfulness

schools.summary: Barnes: what is observed here as worthy of remark is that the whole Jewish people sprang from one man, and that as the reward of his faith — from a single individual, and one whose body was as good as dead; Gill notes 'that is, Abraham'; Paul declaring that from one, as good as dead, sprang a multitude as the stars and the sand — the innumerable seed as the fruit and reward of Abraham's faith (Paul showing the marvellous harvest of that faith: 'therefore sprang there even of one,' from one man, Abraham, 'and him as good as dead,' his body aged and past the power of generation, there came 'so many as the stars of the sky in multitude, and as the sand which is by the sea shore innumerable'; the wonder that from a single, withered source God raised up a countless nation, exactly fulfilling his promise of the stars and the sand, so the very multitude of Abraham's seed stands as the lasting monument of what faith in God's promise can bring to pass).

themes: therefore sprang there even of one, and him as good as dead
so many as the stars of the sky in multitude
and as the sand which is by the sea shore innumerable
the countless seed the monument of faith

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the first virtue, yea the whole of virtue, is to be a stranger to this world and a sojourner, and to have nothing in common with things here but to hang loose from them as from things strange to us, as those blessed disciples did of whom he says 'they wandered about in sheepskins'; Paul declaring that these all died in faith, not having received the promises, but seeing them afar off and confessing themselves strangers and pilgrims on the earth — the dying faith of those who embraced the promise from afar and owned themselves pilgrims (Paul gathering up the patriarchs in a noble summary: 'these all died in faith,' keeping faith to the end and dying as believers, 'not having received the promises,' not seeing the things promised fulfilled in their lifetime, 'but having seen them afar off,' beholding them by faith at a distance, 'and were persuaded of them, and embraced them,' assured of and welcoming them as a man greets a far-off friend, 'and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth'; this open confession that earth was not their home but a place of pilgrimage, their hearts set on the better country).

themes: these all died in faith, not having received the promises
having seen them afar off, and were persuaded of them, and embraced them
confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth
hearts set on the better country

schools.summary: Barnes: they that say such things — that speak of themselves as having come into a land of strangers, and who negotiate only for a small piece of land, not to cultivate but to bury their dead — declare plainly that they seek a country; Paul reasoning that they who say such things declare plainly that they seek a country — the pilgrim-confession proving a homeland sought beyond this world (Paul drawing the inference from the patriarchs' confession: 'they that say such things,' those who own themselves strangers and pilgrims on the earth, 'declare plainly that they seek a country,' for a man does not call himself a stranger and pilgrim unless he has a home elsewhere that he is travelling toward, so their very confession of pilgrimage was an open declaration that they were seeking another country, a true homeland not yet reached, proving that their hearts were set not on what they left or where they wandered but on a country still to be possessed).

themes: for they that say such things
declare plainly that they seek a country
the pilgrim-confession declaring a homeland sought
hearts set on a country still to be possessed

schools.summary: Gill: 'that country' is Chaldea, which was Abraham's country; Barnes: if they had remembered it with sufficient interest and affection to have made them desirous to return, they might have had opportunity to do so; Paul observing that, had they been mindful of the country they left, they might have returned — the proof that their pilgrim-longing was not for the old home but a better (Paul forestalling a possible thought: when he says they sought a country, he does not mean the land they had left, Chaldea, Abraham's old home, for 'if they had been mindful of that country from whence they came out, they might have had opportunity to have returned,' the way back being open and the journey not impossible had they wished it; this proving their hearts were not set on the country behind them, having no homesick desire to go back, but on a different, better country before them, which their pilgrim-confession declared).

themes: if they had been mindful of that country from whence they came out
they might have had opportunity to have returned
their longing not for the old home
hearts set on a better country before them

schools.summary: Gill: this better country is not to be understood of the present time (the patriarchs being not then on earth nor in any third place between heaven and earth, but in heaven), but is the heavenly country they desired; Paul declaring that they desire a better, heavenly country, so that God is not ashamed to be called their God, having prepared for them a city — the heavenly desire honoured by God's owning them and preparing their city (Paul naming the true object of their longing: 'a better country, that is, an heavenly,' not an earthly land at all but heaven itself, the abiding home of God, and marking the high honour God puts on such faith: 'wherefore God is not ashamed to be called their God,' he gladly owning them as his, taking the very title 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob,' 'for he hath prepared for them a city'; God, far from being ashamed of these homeless pilgrims, owning them as his own and having made ready for them the heavenly city they sought, crowning their faith with the everlasting home).

themes: but now they desire a better country, that is, an heavenly
wherefore God is not ashamed to be called their God
for he hath prepared for them a city
the heavenly desire honoured by God's owning them

schools.summary: Chrysostom: great indeed was the faith of Abraham, for while in the case of Abel, Noah, and Enoch there was an opposition of reasonings only and it was necessary to go beyond human reasonings, in this case it was necessary not only to go beyond human reasonings but to manifest something yet greater, faith warring against faith and the command of God against the promise of God; Paul declaring that by faith Abraham, when tried, offered up Isaac, his only begotten son, though he had received the promises — the crowning trial of faith, offering the son of promise at God's command (Paul coming to the supreme test: 'by faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered up Isaac,' at God's command ready to sacrifice his own son, 'and he that had received the promises offered up his only begotten son'; the sharpness of the trial, that Abraham was the very man to whom the promises were given and Isaac the very son in whom they were to be fulfilled, yet he was bidden to offer him up, so God's command seemed to war against God's own promise, and faith met the test, Abraham obeying though it appeared to destroy the promise).

themes: by faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered up Isaac
he that had received the promises offered up his only begotten son
the crowning trial of faith
the command seeming to war against the promise

schools.summary: Barnes: 'of whom it was said, That in Isaac shall thy seed be called' — that is, the posterity promised to Abraham was to descend through Isaac and not through any other; Gill notes it was said to Abraham, of Isaac his own son whom he offered; Paul recalling that of Isaac it was said, 'In Isaac shall thy seed be called' — the sharpness of the trial deepened by Isaac's place in the promise (Paul adding the very word that made the command so testing: God had expressly said of this Isaac, 'In Isaac shall thy seed be called,' the whole promised seed, the nation and the Messiah himself, to come through Isaac and through no other; how this heightened the trial, that to slay Isaac seemed to slay the promise, for if Isaac died childless the word of God would appear to fail, and yet Abraham, holding the command and the promise both, obeyed, trusting God to reconcile what to human sight could not be reconciled).

themes: of whom it was said
That in Isaac shall thy seed be called
the trial deepened by Isaac's place in the promise
to slay him seemed to slay the promise

schools.summary: Gill: Abraham did not go about this affair without thought, and yet did not consult with flesh and blood; his reasoning was the reasoning of faith, and the conclusion of it was not that God would not require it but that God was able to raise him up; Paul explaining that Abraham accounted God able to raise Isaac from the dead, from whence he received him in a figure — the faith that solved the trial by trusting God's power to raise the dead (Paul revealing how Abraham reconciled the command with the promise: 'accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead,' his faith reasoning that, since God had promised the seed through Isaac and yet commanded his death, God must be able and willing to raise Isaac again that the promise might stand, and adding, 'from whence also he received him in a figure,' Abraham in a manner receiving Isaac back from the dead, delivered at the last moment, a figure or type of the resurrection and of Christ, so faith overcame by resting on the God who raises the dead).

themes: accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead
from whence also he received him in a figure
faith trusting God to raise the dead
receiving Isaac back, in a figure, from death

schools.summary: Chrysostom: as Christ said 'many prophets and righteous men have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them' (Matthew 13:17), those righteous men did indeed know the things to come, foreseeing by faith what was far off; Paul declaring that by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things to come — the faith that pronounced prophetic blessings on the unseen future (Paul passing to Isaac: 'by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things to come,' in his blessing of his two sons Isaac speaking not of present things only but prophetically of 'things to come,' the future fortunes of their descendants; this an act of faith, for Isaac, foreseeing nothing by sight, pronouncing by faith the blessings God revealed, assigning to each son his portion in the unseen future, and, in God's overruling, the greater blessing to the younger, Jacob, according to the divine purpose, so faith laid hold of and declared the things God had appointed but not yet shown).

themes: by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau
concerning things to come
faith pronouncing prophetic blessings
declaring what God appointed but had not yet shown

schools.summary: Gill: a dying time was the usual time of blessing with the patriarchs, and the reason was that what was said might be more attended to and regarded and more strongly impressed upon the mind; and this is a proof that it was done in faith by Jacob; Paul declaring that by faith Jacob, when dying, blessed both the sons of Joseph and worshipped, leaning on his staff — the dying faith that blessed and adored at the last (Paul turning to Jacob's deathbed: 'by faith Jacob, when he was a dying, blessed both the sons of Joseph,' at his very end, dim of eye, pronouncing by faith the prophetic blessings on Ephraim and Manasseh, crossing his hands to set the younger before the elder by divine direction, 'and worshipped, leaning upon the top of his staff'; this last act, the dying patriarch, too weak to rise, bowing in worship and adoration of God, supporting himself on his staff, ending his life as he had lived it, in faith and worship, so faith shines brightest at the deathbed, blessing others and adoring God).

themes: by faith Jacob, when he was a dying, blessed both the sons of Joseph
and worshipped, leaning upon the top of his staff
the dying faith that blessed and adored
faith shining brightest at the deathbed

schools.summary: Gill: the riches and honours of Joseph, as they could not secure him from death, so they did not make him unmindful of it, nor was he afraid of dying or uneasy about it, nor did his prosperity make him proud or above speaking to his brethren; Paul declaring that by faith Joseph, when dying, made mention of the departing of Israel and gave commandment concerning his bones — the dying faith that looked to the promised exodus and pledged his bones to Canaan (Paul naming Joseph's deathbed faith: 'when he died,' at the end of his prosperous life in Egypt, he 'made mention of the departing of the children of Israel,' speaking confidently of the coming exodus, that God would surely bring Israel out of Egypt into Canaan as promised, 'and gave commandment concerning his bones,' charging that his bones be carried up to the promised land when they went; this a signal act of faith, Joseph, dying in Egypt amid all its glory, so believing God's promise of Canaan that he bound his very bones to that hope, refusing an Egyptian grave).

themes: by faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel
and gave commandment concerning his bones
the dying faith that looked to the promised exodus
binding his bones to the hope of Canaan

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of the faith of Moses but of the faith of his parents at the time of his birth, which was when Pharaoh had published an edict ordering every male child to be cast into the river, but instead of obeying this order, Moses's parents hid him; Paul declaring that by faith Moses, when born, was hid three months by his parents, who feared not the king's commandment — the faith of parents that defied the king's cruel edict (Paul opening the story of Moses with the faith of his father and mother: 'by faith Moses, when he was born, was hid three months of his parents,' when Pharaoh had decreed the death of every Hebrew boy, they, 'because they saw he was a proper child,' a fair goodly child in whom they discerned something of God's purpose, hiding him in defiance of the edict, 'and they were not afraid of the king's commandment'; the courage of their faith, that, fearing God more than the king, they risked all to preserve the child God had marked, trusting him above the tyrant's death-decree).

themes: by faith Moses, when he was born, was hid three months of his parents
because they saw he was a proper child
they were not afraid of the king's commandment
faith fearing God more than the king

schools.summary: Gill: 'when he was come to years' (or 'was great') — a nobleman in Pharaoh's court, or when he had come to great knowledge being learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, or rather when he was well advanced in years, being full forty years of age; Paul declaring that by faith Moses, when grown, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter — the faith that renounced rank and royalty for God's people (Paul coming to Moses's own faith: 'by faith Moses, when he was come to years,' grown to maturity, learned and great in Egypt, 'refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter,' deliberately turning his back on the rank, wealth, and royal prospects that were his as the adopted son of the princess, renouncing the throne of Egypt itself; the greatness of this faith, that Moses gave up the highest earthly honour by a free and deliberate choice because his faith saw something better in God and his despised people, so faith counts the world's glory loss for the sake of God).

themes: by faith Moses, when he was come to years
refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter
faith renouncing rank and royalty
counting the world's glory loss for God

schools.summary: Gill: the Israelites were God's chosen and peculiar people and the true worshippers of him; Moses chose to be with these, for the company and conversation of such is most eligible to every good man because God is with them; Paul declaring that Moses chose rather to suffer affliction with the people of God than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season — the faith that preferred affliction with God's people to fleeting sinful pleasure (Paul showing the deliberate choice behind Moses's refusal: 'choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God,' freely electing to share the hardship and oppression of the despised Hebrews, 'than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season'; the weighing of his faith: on one side the 'pleasures of sin,' the delights of Egypt's court, sinful because they meant forsaking God's people, yet only 'for a season,' brief and passing, on the other affliction but with God's people, and faith choosing the affliction, judging that suffering with God's people outweighs all the short-lived pleasures of sin).

themes: choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God
than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season
faith choosing affliction over sinful pleasure
suffering with God's people outweighing fleeting pleasure

schools.summary: Gill: 'the reproach of Christ' means reproach for the sake of Christ, who was made known to the Old Testament saints; not any reproach that lay upon Christ as the immediate object, nor upon Israel for the delay of his coming, but reproach borne for Christ and his cause among God's people; Paul declaring that Moses esteemed the reproach of Christ greater riches than Egypt's treasures, having respect to the reward — the faith that prized Christ's reproach above all earthly wealth (Paul giving the inward reckoning of Moses's faith: 'esteeming the reproach of Christ,' the shame and suffering borne for Christ's sake among the despised people of God who looked for the promised Messiah, 'greater riches than the treasures in Egypt,' counting the very reproach endured for Christ a greater wealth than all the vast treasures of the Egyptian empire, and naming the secret of this valuation: 'for he had respect unto the recompence of the reward'; faith looking beyond the present to the reward God gives, and so counting Christ's reproach gain and Egypt's gold loss).

themes: esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt
for he had respect unto the recompence of the reward
faith prizing Christ's reproach above wealth
looking beyond the present to the reward

schools.summary: Gill: 'he forsook Egypt' — either when he fled to Midian (this was before the eating of the passover, and so stands in its proper order, whereas his going out with the children of Israel was after it); the word 'forsook' implies fleeing, and yet it was without fear of the king; Paul declaring that by faith Moses forsook Egypt, not fearing the king's wrath, for he endured as seeing him who is invisible — the faith that braved the king's wrath by fixing the eye on the invisible God (Paul marking the courage of Moses's faith: 'by faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king,' leaving the land undismayed by Pharaoh's fury which would have terrified mere flesh, and giving the secret of his steadfastness: 'for he endured, as seeing him who is invisible'; this striking phrase, that Moses held firm and persevered because, by faith, he kept the invisible God ever before his eye, as real and present as if seen, so faith's power to endure and to defy the visible terrors of kings lies in the steady sight of the unseen God).

themes: by faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king
for he endured, as seeing him who is invisible
faith braving the king's wrath
enduring by the steady sight of the unseen God

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul is wont to establish many things incidentally and is very full of thoughts (for such is the grace of the Spirit), not comprehending a few ideas in a multitude of words but including great and manifold thought in brevity of expressions, as here in the midst of the narrative he weaves in deep matter; Paul declaring that through faith Moses kept the passover and the sprinkling of blood, that the destroyer should not touch the firstborn — the faith that obeyed the passover ordinance and trusted the sheltering blood (Paul naming Moses's faith in the institution of the passover: 'through faith he kept the passover, and the sprinkling of blood,' obeying God's command to slay the lamb and sprinkle its blood on the doorposts, a strange rite to mere reason, 'lest he that destroyed the firstborn should touch them'; this an act of faith, for Moses trusted that the blood, applied as God directed, would shield Israel's firstborn from the destroying angel that smote Egypt, so faith both obeys God's appointed ordinance and rests on the sheltering blood, a type of the blood of Christ by which the believer is sheltered from wrath).

themes: through faith he kept the passover, and the sprinkling of blood
lest he that destroyed the firstborn should touch them
faith obeying the ordinance and trusting the blood
the blood a type of the sheltering blood of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: they passed through the Red Sea, the sea of Suph or weeds, not a river but the sea; by the phrase here used it should seem they passed through (not coming out on the same side), the waters being divided and standing as a wall on either side; Paul declaring that by faith they passed through the Red Sea as by dry land, which the Egyptians attempting were drowned — the faith that walked through the divided sea where unbelief perished (Paul naming the great deliverance at the Red Sea: 'by faith they passed through the Red sea as by dry land,' Israel, at God's command through Moses, walking down into the parted sea, the waters standing as walls, trusting God to bring them safely across on dry ground, 'which the Egyptians assaying to do were drowned'; the contrast, that the same path faith trod safely became the grave of unbelief, for Pharaoh's host, presuming to follow without faith, were swallowed by the returning waters, so faith passes safely where presumption perishes, the sea obeying God to save his people and destroy their foes).

themes: by faith they passed through the Red sea as by dry land
which the Egyptians assaying to do were drowned
faith walking through the divided sea
the path of faith the grave of unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: the walls of Jericho fell down of themselves, not from any natural cause; the Jews say they sank right down into the ground and were swallowed up, even the whole wall falling round about (as the Septuagint expresses it); Paul declaring that by faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after being compassed seven days — the faith that conquered a city by obedience to a strange command (Paul passing to the conquest of Canaan with Jericho: 'by faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were compassed about seven days,' Israel, at God's word through Joshua, marching silently around the fortified city for seven days with the priests and the ark and the trumpets as commanded, and on the seventh day the great walls falling flat of themselves; this the victory of faith and obedience, not of arms, Israel doing nothing but obey a command that to reason seemed foolish, encircling the walls and trusting God, and God himself throwing down the city, so faith conquers by obeying God's word however strange the means).

themes: by faith the walls of Jericho fell down
after they were compassed about seven days
faith conquering by obedience to a strange command
the victory of faith and obedience, not of arms

schools.summary: Gill: the Targum calls Rahab 'a woman that kept a victualling house,' interpreted by Jarchi and Kimchi as 'a seller of food,' and the Hebrew word so explained by a considerable Jewish writer; but she is named the harlot, and her faith is set forth; Paul declaring that by faith the harlot Rahab perished not with the unbelievers, having received the spies with peace — the faith of a Gentile harlot that saved her amid a doomed city (Paul adding a striking example: 'by faith the harlot Rahab perished not with them that believed not,' when Jericho was destroyed and all its unbelieving inhabitants slain Rahab was spared, 'when she had received the spies with peace'; the wonder of this grace and faith, that Rahab, a Gentile and a harlot, hearing of God's mighty works, believed in the God of Israel and proved her faith by receiving and protecting the Israelite spies at the risk of her life, and that faith saved her when all around perished, so faith, even in the most unlikely, lays hold of mercy and is not ashamed).

themes: by faith the harlot Rahab perished not with them that believed not
when she had received the spies with peace
the faith of a Gentile harlot that saved her
faith in the unlikely laying hold of mercy

schools.summary: Gill: Paul intimates he had said enough to prove the definition of faith he had given, and that the elders by it obtained a good report, yet he had not said all he could, having so much to say that he could not say all; Matthew Henry: the apostle, having given a long list of believers, sums up the rest more briefly; Paul saying that time would fail him to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets — the multitude of faith's heroes too many to recount in full (Paul, having traced faith from Abel to the conquest, breaking off the detailed account: 'and what shall I more say? for the time would fail me to tell,' the examples being so many that he cannot expound them all, and sweeping up a further company by name: 'Gedeon, and Barak, and Samson, and Jephthae,' the judges who delivered Israel by faith, 'David also, and Samuel, and the prophets'; this naming, brief as it is, gathering the whole line of Israel's faithful deliverers, kings, and prophets into the roll of faith, showing how rich and long the history of believing men has been).

themes: and what shall I more say? for the time would fail me to tell
of Gedeon, and of Barak, and of Samson, and of Jephthae
of David also, and Samuel, and of the prophets
the heroes of faith too many to recount in full

schools.summary: Gill: 'subdued kingdoms' as David did particularly, who subdued Syria, Moab, Ammon, Amalek, Edom, and the Philistines; war in some cases is lawful, and kingdoms may be subdued, and faith makes use of means to do it; Paul declaring that through faith they subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, and stopped the mouths of lions — the mighty exploits achieved by the power of faith (Paul, instead of telling each story, gathering the great achievements of faith into a series of swift, glorious strokes: 'who through faith subdued kingdoms,' conquered hostile nations as David, 'wrought righteousness,' administered justice and lived godly lives as the judges and kings, 'obtained promises,' received the fulfilment of God's promised blessings, 'stopped the mouths of lions,' as Daniel in the den kept unharmed by faith; every one of these triumphs ascribed not to human strength but to faith, it being through faith that these men did valiantly and won what they won, faith being the secret of all their power).

themes: who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness
obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions
the mighty exploits achieved by faith
every triumph ascribed to faith, not strength

schools.summary: Gill: 'quenched the violence of fire' may be said to be done when a believer or righteous man is delivered out of it (as Lot from Sodom when God rained fire and brimstone), or when by prayer it is stopped (as by Moses at Taberah), or when persons are not hurt by it (as Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the furnace); Paul declaring that through faith they quenched fire, escaped the sword, out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant, and turned the enemy to flight — the deliverances and victories of faith over every danger (Paul continuing the catalogue of faith's triumphs: 'quenched the violence of fire,' as the three Hebrews in the furnace unharmed, 'escaped the edge of the sword,' delivered from death by the sword, 'out of weakness were made strong,' the feeble strengthened as Samson or Hezekiah raised from sickness, 'waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens,' became mighty warriors and routed foreign armies; these too the fruit of faith, which quenched flames, blunted swords, turned weakness into strength, and put whole armies to flight, so there is no danger or enemy that faith, leaning on God, cannot overcome).

themes: quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword
out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant in fight
turned to flight the armies of the aliens
no danger that faith, leaning on God, cannot overcome

schools.summary: Gill: women received their dead raised to life again, as the widow of Zarephath and the Shunammite, whose sons were really dead and received alive again, from the hands of the prophets Elijah and Elisha, in the way of a resurrection and by faith (the faith of the prophets); Paul declaring that women received their dead raised, and others were tortured, not accepting deliverance, that they might obtain a better resurrection — the turn from faith's triumphs to faith's sufferings, both crowned by resurrection hope (Paul, having told faith's victories, now turning to faith's sufferings, marking the change with two contrasting cases: 'women received their dead raised to life again,' the highest of deliverances, the dead given back as by Elijah and Elisha, 'and others were tortured, not accepting deliverance,' others, no less faithful, broken on the rack and refusing release at the price of denying God, 'that they might obtain a better resurrection'; the secret that nerved the martyrs, that they scorned a deliverance that would cost their faith, choosing death because they looked for 'a better resurrection,' the glorious rising of the just, far better than any temporal rescue).

themes: women received their dead raised to life again
others were tortured, not accepting deliverance
that they might obtain a better resurrection
faith nerving the martyrs to refuse deliverance

schools.summary: Gill: others had trial of cruel mockings, as Samson by the Philistines, Elisha by the children whom the bears devoured, Jeremiah by Pashur, the Jews by Sanballat and Tobiah when building the temple, and the prophets whom God sent to the Jews as his messengers; Paul declaring that others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, of bonds and imprisonment — the shame and bonds borne by faith under persecution (Paul continuing the catalogue of faith's sufferings, descending to the harsher trials: 'others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings,' exposed to bitter mockery and the whip as the prophets often were, 'yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment'; these faithful ones bearing not only pain but shame, the cruel mocking that wounds the spirit, the scourging that tears the flesh, and the bonds and prison that rob of liberty, all endured by faith for God's sake, so faith is proved not only in mighty deeds but in patient endurance of contempt, stripes, and chains).

themes: and others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings
yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment
the shame and bonds borne by faith
faith proved in patient endurance of contempt and chains

schools.summary: Chrysostom: at all times, but especially when I reflect upon the achievements of the saints, it comes over me to feel despondency concerning my own condition, because we have not even in dreams experienced the things among which those men spent their whole lives; Paul declaring that they were stoned, sawn asunder, tempted, slain with the sword, and wandered destitute, afflicted, and tormented — the extremity of suffering and death endured by faith (Paul reaching the height of faith's afflictions, the very martyrdoms: 'they were stoned,' as Zechariah and others, 'they were sawn asunder,' as tradition says of Isaiah, 'were tempted,' tried and solicited to forsake God, 'were slain with the sword,' and beyond the killings the lifelong destitution: 'they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins; being destitute, afflicted, tormented'; these enduring the worst the world could do, death in cruel forms and a homeless, hunted, poverty-stricken life, yet holding their faith through all, so faith carries the believer through not only easy victories but the furnace of martyrdom and want).

themes: they were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword
they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins
being destitute, afflicted, tormented
faith holding through the furnace of martyrdom and want

schools.summary: Gill: these words are inserted in a parenthesis to remove or prevent such objections as these — that they were restless and unquiet persons who made disturbance in the world and so were unfit to live in it, and that they were deservedly punished for crimes; rather, the world was not worthy of them; Paul declaring, in a parenthesis, that the world was not worthy of these wandering saints who lived in deserts, mountains, dens, and caves — the surpassing worth of the persecuted faithful whom the world cast out (Paul, in a striking aside, vindicating the honour of these despised sufferers: '(of whom the world was not worthy),' far from being the worthless outcasts the world judged them, these hunted saints being too good for the world that drove them out, the world not deserving to have such men in it, and finishing the picture of their plight: 'they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth,' driven into the wilderness, sheltering in caves like hunted beasts; this reversal of the world's judgment, that those it counted refuse were its true treasure, the world being unworthy of them).

themes: of whom the world was not worthy
they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth
the surpassing worth of the persecuted faithful
the world's judgment reversed

schools.summary: Gill: this may either be limited to the sufferers in the preceding verses, who suffered martyrdom for the faith and are called martyrs or witnesses (as the Ethiopic version), or extended to all the elders mentioned in the chapter; Paul declaring that these all, having obtained a good report through faith, received not the promise — the faithful commended yet not yet given the full promise (Paul gathering up the whole roll in one sweeping observation: 'these all, having obtained a good report through faith,' every one of them from Abel to the martyrs winning God's testimony and approval by their faith, 'received not the promise'; the meaning that, though they were all approved and commended, they did not in their lifetime receive the great promised thing, the coming of Christ and the full salvation he brings, the substance of all the promises, which was reserved for the gospel age, so the Old Testament saints, for all their faith and their good report, looked and longed for what was not given until Christ came).

themes: and these all, having obtained a good report through faith
received not the promise
the faithful commended yet not given the full promise
longing for what was reserved for the gospel age

schools.summary: Gill: this 'better thing' is not a better state of the church free from suffering (for that is the case of saints under the New Testament as under the Old), nor merely the felicity of the soul after death, nor any greater degree of glory, but rather the better thing God provided in the coming of Christ and the gospel, in which the saints of all ages share together; Paul declaring that God provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect — the one perfection shared by Old and New Testament saints together in Christ (Paul closing the great chapter with a profound reason why the ancients received not the promise in their day: 'God having provided some better thing for us,' God in his wisdom keeping back the full blessing, the better thing of Christ's accomplished redemption and the gospel, for 'us,' the New Testament church, 'that they without us should not be made perfect'; the glorious unity of this, that the Old Testament saints were not to reach their final perfection apart from us, but all the people of God, of every age, are to be made perfect together, in one body, through the one Christ, so the faithful of old waited for us, and all the redeemed share at last in one consummated salvation).

debates.question: Why did the Old Testament saints not receive the promise in their day?

sides.position: Because God provided some better thing for the gospel church, that the ancients should not be made perfect apart from us — all the people of God, of every age, being perfected together in one body through the one Christ

themes: God having provided some better thing for us
that they without us should not be made perfect
the one perfection shared by all the saints
all made perfect together in Christ
